title: Lesson 399
transcript: Passwordless authentication. It's exactly what it sounds like. Passwordless authentication is authenticating a user without using a password. So really what passwordless authentication is, is just using the other two factors of authentication to authenticate the user, to verify the user's identity. So authentication by ownership or authentication by characteristic. Authentication by ownership would be examples of having one-time passwords or a YubiKey that you have to have in your possession. That would be a way of authenticating a user without a password. Or biometrics. Authentication by biometrics through say biometrics like fingerprints or retinal scanners. Or authentication through behavioral characteristics like keystroke dynamics. Examples of authentication by characters are things like gate dynamics, how you walk, how you type, how you talk. So how you sign your name. So those would all be authentication by Characteristic, specifically behavioral characteristics. And these are all examples of passwordless authentication. And there's some big benefits of doing this of course, right? It's definitely considered to be more secure. One of the real challenges with passwords is if someone falls victim to say a phishing attack. where they are sent an email telling them that, oh, you know, your bank has decided to give you a $50 free dollars or whatever. Sign into your bank here to claim your $50 free dollars. That's a stupid example, but If a user falls victim to a phishing attack and they enter their username and password on what they think is their real banking website, or what they think is some trusted website, but it's actually the attacker's site, and the attacker has the user's username and password, they can easily now log in as them. That's the problem with passwords, right? If the attacker knows it, they can easily, or use that password to log in as the real user. But the same is not true with authentication by ownership or characteristic. Let's take authentication by ownership with one-time passwords. Those one-time passwords, as the name implies, one time, they're only good once. So even if the attacker captures a user's one-time password, it's going to change. There's going to be a new one-time password in 30 or 60 seconds. And the same thing with biometric data. It's a lot harder to capture and just store and reuse. So passwordless authentication can definitely provide increased security. Users may enjoy it because they don't have to remember these horrible, complex passwords. And as I mentioned, it can definitely provide for reduced phishing. There are, of course, downsides as well, though, right? If someone forgets their phone and they don't have their one-time password generator or their YubiKey, they're locked out. There's also a lot of concerns around biometrics, especially physiological biometrics. the secrecy of someone's biometric data. And if that data is exposed, it's a lot more consequential to the user. So there's some biometric concerns. And these passwordless systems tend to be more expensive. It's not all sunshine, happy days, and rainbows. There's some downsides as well. But overall, it's often a good idea. And that's why you see a lot of organizations looking at ways of authenticating users without using a password.
